You are an LLVM compiler expert specializing in test case generation and bug detection. You are conducting LLVM code review now.

## Context
Maintainers provided a patch, but patches often have edge cases or introduce new issues.

## Your Task
You will work in two phases:
- Phase 1: Analyze the fix to identify potential issues, gaps, and edge cases. Propose test strategies.
- Phase 2: Generate targeted test cases by modifying existing tests to target identified issues

## You Will Receive
- Original patch description and related test case(s)
- The code region that was modified (the patch)
- Information about the optimization pass and component involved

## Tools You Can Use

You have multiple tools for each phase, but you should try to avoid using them with same inputs repeatedly to reduce redundant calls.

**CRITICAL**: You MUST use tool call every action in Phase 1 and Phase 2.

502   /// methods should return the value returned by this function.
503   Instruction *eraseInstFromFunction(Instruction &I) override {
504     LLVM_DEBUG(dbgs() << "IC: ERASE " << I << '\n');
505     assert(I.use_empty() && "Cannot erase instruction that is used!");
506     salvageDebugInfo(I);
507 
508     // Make sure that we reprocess all operands now that we reduced their
509     // use counts.
510     SmallVector<Value *> Ops(I.operands());
511     Worklist.remove(&I);
512     DC.removeValue(&I);
513     I.eraseFromParent();
514     for (Value *Op : Ops)
515       Worklist.handleUseCountDecrement(Op);
516     MadeIRChange = true;
517     return nullptr; // Don't do anything with FI
518   }

231 /// Get the branch weights of a branch conditioned on b1 || b2, where b1 and b2
232 /// are 2 booleans that are the conditions of 2 branches for which we have the
233 /// branch weights B1 and B2, respectively. In both B1 and B2, the first
234 /// position (index 0) is for the 'true' branch, and the second position (index
235 /// 1) is for the 'false' branch.
236 template <typename T1, typename T2,
237           typename = typename std::enable_if<
238               std::is_arithmetic_v<T1> && std::is_arithmetic_v<T2> &&
239               sizeof(T1) <= sizeof(uint64_t) && sizeof(T2) <= sizeof(uint64_t)>>
240 inline SmallVector<uint64_t, 2>
241 getDisjunctionWeights(const SmallVector<T1, 2> &B1,
242                       const SmallVector<T2, 2> &B2) {
243   // For the first conditional branch, the probability the "true" case is taken
244   // is p(b1) = B1[0] / (B1[0] + B1[1]). The "false" case's probability is
245   // p(not b1) = B1[1] / (B1[0] + B1[1]).
246   // Similarly for the second conditional branch and B2.
247   //
248   // The probability of the new branch NOT being taken is:
249   // not P = p((not b1) and (not b2)) =
250   //       = B1[1] / (B1[0]+B1[1]) * B2[1] / (B2[0]+B2[1]) =
251   //       = B1[1] * B2[1] / (B1[0] + B1[1]) * (B2[0] + B2[1])
252   // Then the probability of it being taken is: P = 1 - (not P).
253   // The denominator will be the same as above, and the numerator of P will be:
254   // (B1[0] + B1[1]) * (B2[0] + B2[1]) - B1[1]*B2[1]
255   // Which then reduces to what's shown below (out of the 4 terms coming out of
256   // the product of sums, the subtracted one cancels out).
257   assert(B1.size() == 2);
258   assert(B2.size() == 2);
259   uint64_t FalseWeight = B1[1] * B2[1];
260   uint64_t TrueWeight = B1[0] * (B2[0] + B2[1]) + B1[1] * B2[0];
261   return {TrueWeight, FalseWeight};
262 }
263 } // namespace llvm
264 #endif

.. _i_select:

'``select``' Instruction
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

Syntax:
"""""""

::

      <result> = select [fast-math flags] selty <cond>, <ty> <val1>, <ty> <val2>             ; yields ty

      selty is either i1 or {<N x i1>}

Overview:
"""""""""

The '``select``' instruction is used to choose one value based on a
condition, without IR-level branching.

Arguments:
""""""""""

The '``select``' instruction requires an 'i1' value or a vector of 'i1'
values indicating the condition, and two values of the same :ref:`first
class <t_firstclass>` type.

#. The optional ``fast-math flags`` marker indicates that the select has one or more
   :ref:`fast-math flags <fastmath>`. These are optimization hints to enable
   otherwise unsafe floating-point optimizations. Fast-math flags are only valid
   for selects that return :ref:`supported floating-point types
   <fastmath_return_types>`. Note that the presence of value which would otherwise result
   in poison does not cause the result to be poison if the value is on the non-selected arm.
   If :ref:`fast-math flags <fastmath>` are present, they are only applied to the result,
   not both arms.

Semantics:
""""""""""

If the condition is an i1 and it evaluates to 1, the instruction returns
the first value argument; otherwise, it returns the second value
argument.

If the condition is a vector of i1, then the value arguments must be
vectors of the same size, and the selection is done element by element.

If the condition is an i1 and the value arguments are vectors of the
same size, then an entire vector is selected.

Example:
""""""""

.. code-block:: llvm

      %X = select i1 true, i8 17, i8 42                   ; yields i8:17
      %Y = select nnan i1 true, float 0.0, float NaN      ; yields float:0.0
      %Z = select nnan i1 false, float 0.0, float NaN     ; yields float:poison

Fix Understanding
- The patch changes InstCombine select folding metadata behavior in two places:
  - SPF canonicalization path in `visitSelectInst`: when forming a new select around canonicalized SPF value (e.g., umax/umin/smax/etc), it now passes `MDFrom=&SI` to preserve profile metadata.
  - Logical bool folds in `foldSelectOfBools`: instead of copying old profile metadata into newly created selects/logical forms, it now marks new branch metadata as explicitly unknown (when profiling is enabled), via `CreateSelectWithUnknownProfile`, `createSelectInstWithUnknownProfile`, and `setExplicitlyUnknownBranchWeightsIfProfiled`.
- The intent is to avoid profile verification failures from imprecise propagation when condition structure changes, while preserving metadata in cases deemed semantics-preserving for condition evaluation.

2. Formal Semantic Modeling
- Before fix (relevant paths):
  - Some logical folds reused old select metadata (`MDFrom` from inner/original select), potentially assigning old branch weights to restructured conditions.
  - SPF canonicalization path shown in patch did not explicitly preserve metadata in this location.
- After fix:
  - Logical fold-generated selects are marked `!prof !{!"unknown", !"instcombine"}` (in profiled functions), or left without metadata in unprofiled functions.
  - SPF canonicalization now preserves metadata from original select (`MDFrom=&SI`).
- Potential semantic divergence point:
  - Branch weight metadata semantically models condition true/false likelihood. If transformed select condition differs (even if final value semantics are equivalent), directly copying weights can be imprecise.
- Concrete instantiation:
  - Original: `%r = select (A || B), X, Y` with `P(A)=0.1`, `P(B|!A)=0.9` => `P(A||B)=0.91`.
  - Transformed SPF-like outer form: `select A, X, V(B,X,Y)`.
  - New outer condition true rate is `P(A)=0.1`, not `0.91`. Copying old branch weights to this outer select encodes a different probability model.

3. Assumptions and Preconditions Identified
- Assumption A: In SPF canonicalization site, copying `SI` profile to new select is valid because condition/branch semantics are considered preserved enough.
- Assumption B: In logical fold rewrites, any inherited branch weights are too imprecise, so unknown metadata is the safest correct choice.
- Assumption C: Unknown metadata must only be injected for profiled functions (entry count > 0), otherwise avoid introducing profile metadata.
- Assumption D: All newly created select instructions in affected logical transformations are covered by unknown-marking APIs.

4. Potential Cases to Break Assumptions
- A may break when transformed outer select condition differs materially from original condition probability (short-circuit decomposition forms).
- B may break if one rewrite path still preserves old metadata on one of nested selects while marking another unknown.
- C may break in boundary cases where only branch_weights exist or profile state is unusual (paired profiled/unprofiled variants).
- D may break when builder simplification changes returned value kind (instruction vs constant) or when commuted/vector/scalable matcher path uses a different creation helper.

5. Test Strategies
- Provided above as five targeted strategies, focusing on:
  1) logical nested-fold sanitation,
  2) SPF propagation precision under decomposition,
  3) instruction-kind/folding sensitivity,
  4) profiled vs unprofiled boundary behavior,
  5) vector/scalable parity coverage."}

### Important Rule
You can always keep mutating the test case and verifying it until you find an issue or are confident that no issues can be found. However, make sure it is aligned with the potential issues you identified in Phase 1. Meanwhile, avoid stucking on a test case for too long. If you have tried multiple mutations and still cannot find any issues, move on to other test cases or strategies.

### Handling Verification Results
- **Failed-to-prove**:
  1. Try to reduce the test case to a smaller example that still fails. This can help isolate the specific conditions that cause the issue.
  2. Try to call `difftest` to run the test case with specific input value that you think can trigger the issue based on your analysis. This can help check if the issue can be exposed by actual execution even if it cannot be proved by alive2.
- **Alive2 errors**: Try to call `trans` to run opt with the same command arguments and adjust the test case or command arguments to fix.
- **Correct transformation**: Try to first analyze if the test strategy is correct and then decide to refine the test case or continue to generate other test cases.

### Additional Notes
- **Coverage**: If you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues, you can also find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools.
- **Completeness**: Make sure you have at least explored all the test strategies you proposed in Phase 1, and generated multiple test cases if possible.
- **Completion**: You cannot finish Phase 2 until all tests in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

---

## Tools that you can use

- `findN`: Search for files in the LLVM codebase related to the component or optimization pass to understand the context of the fix and find relevant tests.
- `listN`: List files in the LLVM codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `readN`: Read the content of a file in the LLVM codebase to understand existing tests or the fix.
- `grepN`: Search for specific patterns in the codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `tests_manager`: Manage the list of test cases. You can list all tests to see their status, get the details of a specific test, or mark a test as tested. Your goal is to ensure all tests are tested.
- `trans`: Run the `opt` tool with specific arguments to see how the LLVM IR code is transformed by the optimization pass.
- `verify`: Use alive2 to verify if the transformation from original LLVM IR code to optimized LLVM IR code is correct, which can help check the validity of generated test cases in Phase 2.
- `difftest`: Use llubi to perform differential testing on the original and transformed LLVM IR code, which can help check if the generated test cases cannot be proved by alive2.
- `report`: End Phase 2 by submitting the generated test cases and their verification results.

}

```","opt -S -passes=instcombine"],"thoughts":"Bug Triggering Analysis:
- No miscompile was found across all required tests and mutation strategies.
- This representative SPF case shows expected behavior: InstCombine rewrites to `select %cond, %x, umax(%x,%y)` and preserves branch weights on the resulting outer select. Differential execution matched.

Fix Weakness Analysis:
- We stress-tested all 11 managed tests, and each covered all 5 Phase-1 strategies via targeted mutations:
  1) logical nested-select sanitation,
  2) SPF propagation under decomposition,
  3) instruction-kind/folding sensitivity (`freeze`, no-fold variants),
  4) profiled vs unprofiled boundary,
  5) vector parity checks.
- Observations indicate metadata handling aligns with patch intent:
  - logical restructuring in profiled functions commonly results in `!{!"unknown", !"instcombine"}`;
  - unprofiled paired variants did not gain synthetic unknown metadata;
  - SPF canonicalization preserved weights as intended.
- A few vector constructions hit tool/infra limits (llubi vector crashes and one no-fixpoint verifier issue requiring `instcombine<no-verify-fixpoint>`), but no semantic divergence between original and transformed IR was observed in completed differential checks."}
